heading: Military medals or decorations
label: 704 Military medals or decorations
notes: 1948ActBased on , U.S.C., 1940 ed., Army and Air Force (, ; , ).section 1425 of title 10Feb. 24, 1923, ch. 11042 Stat. 1286Apr. 21, 1928, ch. 39245 Stat. 437Section was made to cover the decorations and medals of the Navy Department as well as the War Department.Minor changes were made in phraseology.

1949ActThis section [section 16] clarifies the wording of , U.S.C., to embrace all service decorations awarded to members of the armed forces whether by the Army, Navy, Air Force, or other branch of such forces. (See note to sec. 5 [of 1949 Act, set out in Legislative History note under ]).section 704 of title 18section 244 of title 18

Findings, , , provided that:Pub. L. 109–437, § 2120 Stat. 3266Dec. 20, 2006“Congress makes the following findings:“(1)Fraudulent claims surrounding the receipt of the Medal of Honor, the distinguished-service cross, the Navy cross, the Air Force cross, the Purple Heart, and other decorations and medals awarded by the President or the Armed Forces of the United States damage the reputation and meaning of such decorations and medals.“(2)Federal law enforcement officers have limited ability to prosecute fraudulent claims of receipt of military decorations and medals.“(3)Legislative action is necessary to permit law enforcement officers to protect the reputation and meaning of military decorations and medals.”

Findings, , , provided that:Pub. L. 109–437, § 2120 Stat. 3266Dec. 20, 2006“Congress makes the following findings:“(1)Fraudulent claims surrounding the receipt of the Medal of Honor, the distinguished-service cross, the Navy cross, the Air Force cross, the Purple Heart, and other decorations and medals awarded by the President or the Armed Forces of the United States damage the reputation and meaning of such decorations and medals.“(2)Federal law enforcement officers have limited ability to prosecute fraudulent claims of receipt of military decorations and medals.“(3)Legislative action is necessary to permit law enforcement officers to protect the reputation and meaning of military decorations and medals.”

children.heading: .—In General
children.label: a .—In General
children.text: Whoever knowingly purchases, attempts to purchase, solicits for purchase, mails, ships, imports, exports, produces blank certificates of receipt for, manufactures, sells, attempts to sell, advertises for sale, trades, barters, or exchanges for anything of value any decoration or medal authorized by Congress for the armed forces of the United States, or any of the service medals or badges awarded to the members of such forces, or the ribbon, button, or rosette of any such badge, decoration or medal, or any colorable imitation thereof, except when authorized under regulations made pursuant to law, shall be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than six months, or both.

children.heading: .—Fraudulent Representations About Receipt of Military Decorations or Medals
children.label: b .—Fraudulent Representations About Receipt of Military Decorations or Medals
children.text: Whoever, with intent to obtain money, property, or other tangible benefit, fraudulently holds oneself out to be a recipient of a decoration or medal described in subsection (c)(2) or (d) shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than one year, or both.

children.heading: Enhanced Penalty for Offenses Involving Congressional Medal of Honor.—
children.label: c Enhanced Penalty for Offenses Involving Congressional Medal of Honor.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: If a decoration or medal involved in an offense under subsection (a) is a Congressional Medal of Honor, in lieu of the punishment provided in that subsection, the offender shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 1 year, or both.

children.label: A
children.text: a medal of honor awarded under section 7271, 8291, or 9271 of title 10 or section 491 of title 14;1See References in Text note below.1

children.label: B
children.text: a duplicate medal of honor issued under section 7284, 8306, or 9284 of title 10 or section 504 of title 14; or1

children.label: C
children.text: a replacement of a medal of honor provided under section 7277, 8303, or 9277 of title 10 or section 501 of title 14.1

children.heading: Enhanced Penalty for Offenses Involving Certain Other Medals.—
children.label: d Enhanced Penalty for Offenses Involving Certain Other Medals.—

children.heading: .—In general
children.label: 1 .—In general
children.text: If a decoration or medal involved in an offense described in subsection (a) is a distinguished-service cross awarded under , a Navy cross awarded under , an Air Force cross awarded under , a silver star awarded under section 7276, 8294, or 9276 of title 10, a Purple Heart awarded under , a combat badge, or any replacement or duplicate medal for such medal as authorized by law, in lieu of the punishment provided in the applicable subsection, the offender shall be fined under this title, imprisoned not more than 1 year, or both.section 7272 of title 10section 8292 of title 10section 9272 of title 10section 1129 of title 10

children.heading: .—Combat badge defined
children.label: 2 .—Combat badge defined
children.text: In this subsection, the term “combat badge” means a Combat Infantryman’s Badge, Combat Action Badge, Combat Medical Badge, Combat Action Ribbon, or Combat Action Medal.
